# Web Boundary Tests — Zone Context

<zone>

Zone: Web Boundary Tests (`web-boundary-tests`)
Files: 2, Cohesion: 1.00, Coupling: 0.00
Risk: healthy (score: 0.00)
Description: Integration test suite enforcing the web package's architectural contracts: zone import boundaries and build output shape.

</zone>

<files>

packages/web/tests/integration/boundary-check.test.ts (TypeScript, 140 lines, test)
packages/web/tests/integration/build-output-contract.test.ts (TypeScript, 177 lines, test)

</files>

<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Pairing boundary-check.test.ts with build-output-contract.test.ts in the same zone is strong architectural practice — source-level and artifact-level contracts are enforced together, preventing regression at both layers.
[observation] [info] This zone is exclusively test files, consistent with the '-tests' naming convention; no production source files are present, so the classification is accurate.
[observation] [info] Zero outbound imports into production zones is the correct property for a policy-test zone — any future test added here that imports directly from viewer or server source files should be scrutinized to ensure it remains a structural test rather than a feature test.
[pattern] [warning] Neither boundary-check nor build-output-contract validates that web-viewer's inbound imports to viewer sub-zones (crash, panel) enter only through declared barrel files — internal encapsulation of viewer sub-zones is outside the current test scope
[pattern] [info] web-boundary-tests is the only zone-local boundary enforcement in the monorepo — hench, rex, and sourcevision packages rely solely on the e2e architecture-policy test suite for import boundary enforcement, creating a coverage asymmetry between the web package and domain packages
[anti-pattern] [warning] The two test files in web-boundary-tests do not assert on import paths within viewer sub-zones (crash, panel) — specifically, whether web-viewer's 2 inbound imports to crash target only crash/index.ts barrel and whether hench-runs.ts imports panels through a barrel; these gaps mean sub-zone encapsulation regressions are invisible to the entire enforcement suite
[suggestion] [warning] Add a boundary-check assertion that files importing hench-agent-monitor panel components (active-tasks-panel, concurrency-panel, memory-panel, ws-health-panel, throttle-controls) must do so via components/index.ts rather than direct file paths — this enforces the barrel contract that already exists but is currently bypassed by hench-runs.ts.
[suggestion] [info] Replace 'export * from "./constants.js"' in components/index.ts with named re-exports so the barrel's complete public surface is visible at a glance and any changes to constants.js exports become explicit edits to the barrel.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Two complementary tests together cover both the source-level boundary rules (boundary-check) and the compiled artifact contract (build-output-contract), forming a complete architectural guard
- Cohesion of 1.0 confirms these tests are tightly coupled to each other conceptually — they are both policy enforcement tests, not feature tests
- Zero coupling to other zones is correct: policy tests should depend on build artifacts and static analysis, not on the modules they police
- Pairing boundary-check.test.ts with build-output-contract.test.ts in the same zone is strong architectural practice — source-level and artifact-level contracts are enforced together, preventing regression at both layers.
- This zone is exclusively test files, consistent with the '-tests' naming convention; no production source files are present, so the classification is accurate.
- Zero outbound imports into production zones is the correct property for a policy-test zone — any future test added here that imports directly from viewer or server source files should be scrutinized to ensure it remains a structural test rather than a feature test.
- This zone is the sole import-policy enforcement point for the entire web package — no equivalent zone-specific boundary tests exist for hench, rex, or sourcevision packages within their own test suites; policy violations in those packages are only caught by the broader e2e architecture-policy tests
- The two tests cover complementary enforcement layers (source import rules + compiled artifact contracts) but neither test validates that viewer sub-zones (crash, panel) respect their own internal encapsulation boundaries — sub-zone barrel hygiene is currently unchecked
- web-boundary-tests is the only zone-local boundary enforcement in the monorepo — hench, rex, and sourcevision packages rely solely on the e2e architecture-policy test suite for import boundary enforcement, creating a coverage asymmetry between the web package and domain packages
- Neither boundary-check nor build-output-contract validates that web-viewer's inbound imports to viewer sub-zones (crash, panel) enter only through declared barrel files — internal encapsulation of viewer sub-zones is outside the current test scope
- web-boundary-tests has cohesion 1.0 and zero coupling but enforces rules that govern 9+ production zones — its scope-to-size ratio (2 test files governing the entire web package) means any gap in its assertions goes undetected across all zones simultaneously; adding a third file for sub-zone barrel enforcement would increase coverage without diluting the zone's focused purpose
- The two test files in web-boundary-tests do not assert on import paths within viewer sub-zones (crash, panel) — specifically, whether web-viewer's 2 inbound imports to crash target only crash/index.ts barrel and whether hench-runs.ts imports panels through a barrel; these gaps mean sub-zone encapsulation regressions are invisible to the entire enforcement suite
- The components/index.ts barrel in src/viewer/components/ is already established, yet no assertion in boundary-check.test.ts verifies that consumers of viewer components (particularly hench-runs.ts) import from the barrel rather than directly from individual component files. Six direct component imports in hench-runs.ts currently escape all boundary enforcement undetected.
- components/index.ts uses 'export * from "./constants.js"' (line 86) as its only wildcard export, while all other 28 exports use named re-exports. This inconsistency in export style makes the barrel's public surface partially opaque — wildcard exports hide what symbols constants.js contributes to the barrel, and a change to constants.js could silently add or remove exported names without any barrel update.
- Add a boundary-check assertion that files importing hench-agent-monitor panel components (active-tasks-panel, concurrency-panel, memory-panel, ws-health-panel, throttle-controls) must do so via components/index.ts rather than direct file paths — this enforces the barrel contract that already exists but is currently bypassed by hench-runs.ts.
- Replace 'export * from "./constants.js"' in components/index.ts with named re-exports so the barrel's complete public surface is visible at a glance and any changes to constants.js exports become explicit edits to the barrel.
- [call graph] 7 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
